Data.MeaningHint: Bamboo, interval, bamboo, interval, bamboo… Almost like a binary rhythm: 1, 0, 1, 0, 1. Now that's simplicity. It doesn't get simpler than that!
Data.ReadingHint: Simplicity is how he took over the world. Attack or don't attack? Boom, simple. If he hadn't stuck to simplicity, he wouldn't have been able to do what he did. Complex thoughts just get in the way of expansionist ambitions, you know?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: That kind of <kanji>simplicity</kanji> is what Genghis <reading>Khan</reading> (かん) always demanded. Everything he decided was based on simplicity.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You line up stalks of <radical>bamboo</radical> with an equal <radical>interval</radical> between each one. This pattern creates a sense of <kanji>simplicity</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Why hadn't anyone thought of making a samurai magazine before?!
Data.ReadingHint: The sheep samurai is ruining your magazine! Maybe you should start a new magazine called, "All the places the sheep samurai will be, so stay away!" to make up for all the damage you've caused.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, your <kanji>magazine</kanji> helps the <reading>shee</reading>p (し) samurai find other samurai... and kill them. None of the samurai want to share their feelings with your magazine anymore, oh no!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: To <radical>say</radical> what's in a <radical>samurai</radical>'s <radical>heart</radical> you start a <kanji>magazine</kanji>. You go around writing about and interviewing all the samurai so you can share what they say to everyone who wants to read!

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine someone looking into a hole that shows your private heart. That's a window. A window you probably should have kept closed. Now everyone is looking in.
Data.ReadingHint: Look out your window back at the faces. Try to express how mad you are at them with your face through the glass. Make as many mad faces that you can think of.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You realize that a ton of people are looking into your <kanji>window</kanji> and seeing all your private things. This makes you <reading>mad</reading> (まど)! What right do they have to know your private thoughts and desires?!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>hole</radical> leading to the <radical>private</radical> <radical>heart</radical> of your house is the <kanji>window</kanji>. Or maybe it's just the window to your soul, and not your house. Like the poetry people say.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine encountering this Not Mouth. Ask it a question. It answers, “No”. Imagine doing this over and over until you believe that the Not Mouth always says “no”.
Data.ReadingHint: Listen as the Not Mouth continues to scream in bloodcurdling agony. Hear the flames flicker as they lick across the Not Mouth’s being. Seems as though the heat was too much for the Not Mouth.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You ask the Not Mouth if it is afraid of <reading>he</reading>at (ひ). The Not Mouth says, “<kanji>no</kanji>”. So you set the Not Mouth on fire. The Not Mouth then screams, “NONONONONONONONONONOOOOOO~!” Looks like someone can’t handle the heat.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>Not</radical> <radical>Mouth</radical> says “<kanji>no</kanji>”. What else would you expect from something named the “Not Mouth”? The Not Mouth will always say “no”, regardless of the question.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture your new bamboo muscles glowing under your skin with the moon's power. You flex and feel the power coursing through you!
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine standing before the King, explaining in detail how you cut yourself open and replaced your weak human meat with the special muscles you have now. He's staring intently at you, but more so at your shining body than your mouth as you talk.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Your special <kanji>muscle</kanji>s and <kanji>tendon</kanji>s have become famous and the <reading>Kin</reading>g (きん) of England has requested that you appear in his court! It looks like he wants you to teach his barber surgeons how you got your new muscles.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: This <radical>bamboo</radical> absorbed the <radical>moon</radical>'s <radical>power</radical>, and you want that power in your body, so you surgically insert them into your body to replace your <kanji>muscle</kanji>s and <kanji>tendon</kanji>s.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a shadowy shape on top of the roof, proclaiming to the entire town that the new day is here! It's come! And it's also time to get up.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture that shadow on the roof, proclaiming it's a new day, but this time make sure you picture the outline of this centaur. He has a human top half and a horse bottom half. And he's yelling down at you, "HEY THE SUN IS UP IT'S DAWN GET OUT OF BED!"
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The thing that climbs up on the roof to <kanji>proclaim</kanji> that the new day is here is a <reading>cen</reading>taur (せん). Ohoho, did you think I was going to say it was a rooster? Wrong! Centaurs are taller than humans and roosters, so when they climb onto the roof with their horse legs they can see dawn starting before anyone else.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Someone climbs onto the <radical>roof</radical> from the <radical>ground</radical> when <radical>dawn</radical> comes to <kanji>proclaim</kanji> that a new day has begun! They proclaim this at the top of their lungs!

Data.MeaningHint: Look at one of these revered horns. Pick it up and feel its weight. It measures very large. Your arms are getting tired from the weight.
Data.ReadingHint: Good thing you already have one of these revered horns. Now imagine yourself grinding it up into a powder to mix into a potion for your son to drink. Hear the horn grinding away as you physically break the horn up into millions of tiny pieces.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The reason you are interested in these <kanji>revered</kanji> horns so much is that your <reading>son</reading> (そん) is sick and you need one of these revered horns to make a medicine for your son. Only with the revered power of the horns will you be able to save your son’s life.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>horns</radical> of the creatures who drink <radical>alcohol</radical> have a <radical>measurement</radical> so large that they are <kanji>revered</kanji> the world over. The horns are revered both for their size and potency. The horns can be used in medicine, so they are very valuable, those alcoholic monster horns.

Data.MeaningHint: Who do you feel the most loyalty towards? Imagine them in the middle of your heart. Feel them in your chest. Imagine them, literally inside the middle of your heart.
Data.ReadingHint: Chewbacca is very loyal. Focus on your loyalty to Chewbacca. Picture him right in the middle of your heart and hear his Chewbacca yell. Also keep in mind that this kanji shares its reading with 中 which is also included in this kanji.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You pictured <reading>Chew</reading>bacca (ちゅう), didn’t you? No? Well you should have. Chewbacca deserves everyone’s <kanji>loyalty</kanji>. Chewbacca is super loyal to you, so he deserves your loyalty back.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Those that are in the <radical>middle</radical> of your <radical>heart</radical> are the ones you have the most <kanji>loyalty</kanji> towards. The middle of your heart is the most sacred, special place for people, so it makes sense that those you keep in the middle of your heart are those you are most loyal to.

Data.MeaningHint: Extend your fingers right now and see how wide you can make them.
Data.ReadingHint: You poked the cock right in the eye with your extended fingers. Maybe you shouldn't be doing that in public. Innocent cocks could get hurt.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you <kanji>extend</kanji> your fingers a <reading>cock</reading> (かく) comes running by and runs straight into them. Bu-COCK!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: To make your <radical>fingers</radical> <radical>wide</radical> you <kanji>extend</kanji> them.

Data.MeaningHint: "Ah golly gee wiz, he sure got his fingers all up in my syrup again, dagnabbit." You know, this saying we say all the time when someone manipulates us into doing something.
Data.ReadingHint: What happens to your soul, you ask? It gets covered in syrup. That's probably where the syrup fingers phrase came from in the first place.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: If you want to <kanji>manipulate</kanji> someone else it's going to have a permanent effect on your <reading>sou</reading>l (そう). Being manipulative isn't cool. And your soul will suffer forever for it.
Data.MeaningMnemonic:
If someone has their <radical>fingers</radical> in your <radical>syrup</radical>, it's just another way of saying they <kanji>manipulate</kanji>d you. Cause only slimy people manipulate other people, so their fingers are slimy and sticky because they're covered in your syrup from mucking up your life.

This also has another meaning: <kanji>chastity</kanji>. Just imagine if someone wanted you to keep your chastity and they called s.e.x. when someone "puts their fingers in your syrup." That would be pretty effective, right? Cause it's like, super gross.

Data.MeaningHint: Even better, write a poem about winter IN winter. Then they'll really respect you.
Data.ReadingHint: The cake has the word RESPECT in fancy, cursive letters. Imagine eating that cake and taste the respect of your poet peers. Mm mm!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Once you have their <kanji>respect</kanji> you get <reading>ca</reading>ke (けい)!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Writing a <radical>poem</radical> about <radical>winter</radical> is the only way to get <kanji>respect</kanji> from other poets.

Data.MeaningHint: Just feel happy that your plants have such a nice livelihood now that the sun is out. They look so healthy and fresh!
Data.ReadingHint: Look at Bobo do what he needs to do to maintain his own livelihood. Hear the flowers screaming as they are plucked from their homes.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: From out of nowhere, <reading>Bo</reading>bo (ぼ) the notorious clown thief starts picking the plants, ending their <kanji>livelihood</kanji>. Bobo needs the flowers for his clown acts, so he uses the livelihood of the flowers to support his own livelihood.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>greenhouse</radical> is already really good when it comes to a plant's livelihood. But add the <radical>sun</radical> into that equation and you have something really great for the plants. Their <kanji>livelihood</kanji> will be awesome, now!

Data.MeaningHint: See the light from the fire as the ashes fly off the cliff. The wind carries some of the ashes over to you. Feel the ashes as they strike your face and wipe the black ash with your hands.
Data.ReadingHint: Ashes on my face. Falling from the cliff of fire. Gives my life meaning. Or just come up with your own haiku. Whatever works for you.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The <kanji>ashes</kanji> inspire you to write a <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい). Using the ashes you write a haiku on a rock for everyone to enjoy.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: On a <radical>cliff</radical>, a <radical>fire</radical> burns to <kanji>ashes</kanji>. The ashes then blow off the cliff, and follow the wind wherever it may go.

Data.MeaningHint: Picture yourself receiving something, arranging it in a circle in the pan, and leaving it to boil. See how quickly that stuff ripens? Now it’s all ripe and yummy.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine the jukebox playing music to accompany your ripened treat. Ahh, jukebox music and ripened goodies. Can life get any sweeter?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Once everything you received has <kanji>ripen</kanji>ed, you kick back near your <reading>juke</reading>box (じゅく) and eat it. The jukebox sets the mood, making the ripened treat feels twice as sweet.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>receive</radical> something edible, arrange it in a <radical>circle</radical> and <radical>boil</radical> it. That way, it’ll <kanji>ripen</kanji> more quickly.

Data.MeaningHint: Try to picture how each rice paddy differs from the next. Can you think of any differences in the ones you've seen?
Data.ReadingHint: Aha! As an eagle you can easily see that all of the rice paddies have differing colors, shapes, and (if you fly low) smells. They differ a lot!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: To see how rice paddies <kanji>differ</kanji>, you need an <reading>ea</reading>gle's (い) eye view. It can be hard to see from the ground, so soar up into the sky like an eagle and look down at the rice paddies together.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Put a <radical>rice paddy</radical> <radical>together</radical> with other paddies and you'll see how much they <kanji>differ</kanji>. When you're driving or taking a train through the countryside it may seem like all of those rice paddies blurring together are all the same, but they can differ quite a bit!

Data.MeaningHint: See the emperor, garbed all in white. There are other kings, but the emperor's white clothing shows his status above the rest.
Data.ReadingHint:
How did こういち become emperor?! His first decree as emperor is that everybody must learn kanji now.

Note: from now on, Emperor こういち will be a recurring character for the reading mnemonic of こう.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Who is the <kanji>emperor</kanji>? It is <reading>こう</reading>いち. こういち the emperor stands over his subjects.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Social commentary aside, the <radical>white</radical> <radical>king</radical> is the king that wears all white. Traditionally, this is someone better than a king. The <kanji>emperor</kanji>!

Data.MeaningHint: If you have piles of something, you're prosperous. If you become a plate, that's the best thing that can happen to you.
Data.ReadingHint: You know a family is seriously prosperous when they don't use forks, they use a magical saber to cut their food. Wowee wow wow.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You're so <kanji>prosperous</kanji> you use your <reading>sa</reading>ber (せい) to cut the food pile and shove the bits into your plate mouth. In fact, your entire family is using your special saber to cut and eat off of you. A normal knife wouldn't be able to get through all this food!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you <radical>become</radical> a <radical>plate</radical>, I will <kanji>pile</kanji> food up upon you. With such a huge pile, everyone will think you're <kanji>prosperous</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Feel these few grains of sand between your fingertips. Imagine the stones that they originally came from. Nature is crazy.
Data.ReadingHint: You may wonder what a sand saw is. Well, it's a saw for cutting sand into even finer little pieces. How does it work? It's a mystery!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: After breaking up your stone into <kanji>sand</kanji>, you fetch your special sand <reading>sa</reading>w (さ). A few bits are still too chunky, so you'll have to use a sand saw now.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you grind a <radical>stone</radical> down to more than just a <radical>few</radical> pieces, it will become <kanji>sand</kanji>. Stones that get broken up enough will become sand, and you pick up a few grains in your hand.

Data.MeaningHint: Taste that sweet, sweet sugar. Not many people know, but this is how it's made. The canopy is needed for temperature control, and the wolverine… well, it's got special enzymes in its mouth or something.
Data.ReadingHint: As you eat the sugar, you picture とうきょう. The place where you fell in love with these sugar-making techniques.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You learned how to make this kind of <kanji>sugar</kanji> in <reading>とう</reading>きょう. You found a special とうきょう sugar sensei and they introduced you to all the secret methods you know now.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Bring some <radical>rice</radical> under a <radical>canopy</radical> and stuff it in <radical>Wolverine</radical>'s <radical>mouth</radical>. Make him chew it a bit, scrape it out, and voila — you've made <kanji>sugar</kanji>!

Data.MeaningHint: Go inside your house or shed, it doesn't matter where, and look happily at your thread supply. You have thread in all sizes, shapes, and especially colors.
Data.ReadingHint: Nostradamus says you should have a large supply of everything you use regularly. Thread, water, canned foods. You can't see the future, but he can, and if he's telling you to stock up on your inside supplies I think you should listen.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You have this <kanji>supply</kanji> because <reading>No</reading>stradamus (のう) had a prediction, saying you would. And that if you didn't keep a good supply of thread inside, you'd run out.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>thread</radical> you have <radical>inside</radical> is your whole <kanji>supply</kanji>. When you're outside sewing and you run out, you just go inside, look through your supply, and pick a new one.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine having a moon and a city inside your lung. Feel their presence as you breathe in and breathe out. Also, 月 often refers to internal organs as well, so remember that when you see this kanji.
Data.ReadingHint:
The city and moon
Inside my lung, forgotten.
Where is the answer?

Or just think of your own haiku. Be creative!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Having a moon and city inside your <kanji>lung</kanji> inspires you to write a <reading>hai</reading>ku (はい). Perhaps the city and moon are just metaphorical. Either way, a haiku is the best way to go about things here.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: There is a <radical>moon</radical> and a <radical>city</radical> inside your <kanji>lung</kanji>. Inside of your lung there is a city and above that city lies a moon. It’s a pretty sight, but also very strange. Moons and cities don’t usually reside inside lungs.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine a time when a book you were reading used flowery language and you couldn't understand. Imagine the annoyance with the author for being so overwrought and flowery.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the author trying to use chopsticks to hold some little flowers. It looks like they're having a hard time, but people will do anything to be a great author, I suppose.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Unfortunately, the <kanji>author</kanji> is allergic to touching flowers, so they hold them with a pair of <reading>cho</reading>psticks (ちょ). They hold them real close to their face with the chopsticks, so they can still use them to think of fancy, author-ly words.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you want to give <radical>flowers</radical> to <radical>someone</radical>, give them to an <kanji>author</kanji>. Authors really love flowers, because they help them think of flowery language they can use to sound smart in their books.

Data.MeaningHint: Steam comes out of the coral flowers because they hold a lot of water. They're flowers, but they're coral flowers, so they came from the ocean. Of course there's water in them, and if you boil that water steam will come out.
Data.ReadingHint: It's weird that Joe is so sensitive to the smell of steaming coral. He's lived as a farmhand nowhere near the sea all his life... at least that's what he told you. And now he's in your house breathing in the steam really deeply with his nose practically in the pot.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: As you watch the <kanji>steam</kanji> coming off the coral flowers, someone appears behind you. It’s <reading>Joe</reading> (じょう), the big farmhand. "Are you steaming coral?" he asks. "Smelled it a mile away. I love steaming me some coral.”
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you take the <radical>flowers</radical> off some <radical>coral</radical> and <radical>boil</radical> them, <kanji>steam</kanji> comes out.

Data.MeaningHint: Your brain is like a storehouse, you know? Hopefully that's where you kept your storehouse knowledge.
Data.ReadingHint: Be really proud of your storehouse. You've collected a lot of zombies and put them in there, where they'll keep fresh a long time!
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You have a <kanji>storehouse</kanji>. Inside is where you keep all your <reading>zo</reading>mbies (ぞう).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>storehouse</radical> radical and the <kanji>storehouse</kanji> kanji meanings are the same. If you know the radical, you know the kanji!

Data.MeaningHint: Picture a samurai in his winter attire. His helmet is white and fuzzy and he has decorative icicles hanging down off of his armor.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture the kind of attire you'd wear as a samurai to reflect your soul. Mine would be bright pink and covered in horses and bananas and daisies to show that I have a happy, kind soul and that I don't want to be killed by anyone else.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: <kanji>Attire</kanji> like this is important because it reflects your <reading>soul</reading> (そう). The samurai is wearing clothing with icicles because that's how his heart feels too: frozen over because he has to fight in this horrible winter weather.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: If you see <radical>icicle</radical>s hanging off of a <radical>samurai</radical>'s <radical>clothes</radical>, don't worry, those are just part of his winter <kanji>attire</kanji>. Samurai have to travel and fight in all sorts of conditions, so it only makes sense that they have attire for every season.

Data.MeaningHint: You've been curious about what's on the lid (and your village's) backside for years, and you finally can't take it anymore so you kick the lid as hard as you can!
Data.ReadingHint: Wait, isn't uranium bad for people? This explains why all of the villagers' backsides are covered in weird glowing, green lumps.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: It turns out your village's own <kanji>backside</kanji> is covered in <reading>ura</reading>nium (うら). Eureka! You're all gonna be rich!
Data.MeaningMnemonic: There's a <radical>lid</radical> on your <radical>village</radical>, so you <radical>kick</radical> it over on its <kanji>backside</kanji> to see what's on its <kanji>underside</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: You are someone who can say things in various languages. Speak some of these various languages now. You are someone who is good at saying things. Nice job.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine yourself at this show. The spotlight is on and all eyes are watching. You feel kind of nervous, but you speak the various languages with ease.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: You’re so good at speaking <kanji>various</kanji> languages that you decide to put on a <reading>sho</reading>w (しょ). At the show, you say a lot of things in various languages and everyone is impressed.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: They <radical>say</radical> <radical>someone</radical> with <kanji>various</kanji> talents is important. Various talents can include speaking various languages so if you are someone who knows various languages you can say things that various peoples will comprehend.

Data.MeaningHint: King Shellfish loves when people rent from him. Imagine him rolling in all of the rent money (shellfish) he collected from all the people who rent from him. Hear his shell clattering as he frolics on all the dead shells from the shellfish.
Data.ReadingHint: Feel your entire body shaking, but especially your chin, as you tell him you're two shellfish short on rent this month. The last thing you see is his big fist coming right for your chin.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: If you don't have enough shellfish to pay your <kanji>rent</kanji>, King Shellfish will punch you in the <reading>chin</reading> (ちん).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: The <radical>leader</radical> who is <radical>king</radical> of every <radical>shellfish</radical> sets the <kanji>rent</kanji> for everyone. If you rent a home in King Shellfish's kingdom, the rent will be set by the great shellfish leader himself.

Data.MeaningHint: Just imagine these fins forcing your mouth to move with all their flapping. This is what happens when you have too much alcohol, you can't control yourself.
Data.ReadingHint: Just imagine trying to talk eloquently and clearly when you have fins flapping around your mouth. And if you've had a few drinks on top of that, it stands to reason that you'd start slurring your words.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: The flapping fins are making you slur your words, but that doesn't stop you from spreading <kanji>rumors</kanji> and <kanji>gossip</kanji>: "<reading>Ooo</reading> <reading>whassa</reading>t (うわさ) I hear about so and so having an affair?!"
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Next to your <radical>mouth</radical> there are <radical>fins</radical> which react to <radical>alcohol</radical>. If you exceed a certain alcohol <radical>measurement</radical>, they start flapping about, which makes you spread <kanji>rumors</kanji> and <kanji>gossip</kanji> uncontrollably.

Data.MeaningHint: Your moon weapon is a moon that you keep in your crotch. We'll let you imagine how this one works...
Data.ReadingHint: Your mother had a moon weapon, and her father before her. It's been in the family for generations, and you hope to pass it on to your <reading>子</reading> as well.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>crotch</kanji> has a moon weapon, and it's served you well, so you hope that if you have a <reading>子 </reading> (こ) one day, they'll have one too.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: You keep your <radical>moon</radical> <radical>weapon</radical> in your <kanji>crotch</kanji>. Where else would you keep it?

Data.MeaningHint: How do you compare your flatulence? I recommend a flag. You can see which fart is strongest by seeing how much the flag flutters.
Data.ReadingHint: Picture yourself unable to stop snickering over the word "flatulence", like a little schoolchild. Heh… heh…
Data.ReadingMnemonic:
How do you read <kanji>flatulence</kanji>? <reading>Heh</reading>(へ)...heh..heh..heheheh. You can't NOT laugh at the word flatulence.

Note that some dictionaries incorrectly list おなら as a reading for this kanji. While おなら also means "flatulence" (as in "fart"), you’ll only ever encounter this reading in hiragana, as the kanji can only be read as へ.
Data.MeaningMnemonic: Use a <radical>flag</radical> to <radical>compare</radical> your <kanji>flatulence</kanji>.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine your leader wearing knee-high leather boots. Wouldn't you feel a little more confident in a leader with boots like that?
Data.ReadingHint: Your leader must've already been on the verge of becoming king if the slight advantage from those boots was enough to tip the scales.
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Getting that <kanji>slight</kanji> edge over other leaders allows your leader to become the <reading>kin</reading>g (きん).
Data.MeaningMnemonic: A <radical>leader</radical> in <radical>boot</radical>s has a <kanji>slight</kanji> edge over one without boots. It's not much, but even <kanji>a little</kanji> advantage is better than nothing.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine you're that someone: feel the drops of sweat rolling down your brow as your leader explains their money-making plan to you. It's a good plan — you just hope you can carry it out properly and bring back some money for your leader! (Note that the drop radical can be dropped sometimes — that is, it's fairly common for it to be omitted in handwriting or in certain fonts.)
Data.ReadingHint: You do a great job and pretty soon you're mowing lawns all over town, raking in profits hand over fist. Before you know it, you've built a full-blown money-making mow-nopoly, and your leader couldn't be prouder. Guess you didn't need to be so nervous about succeeding after all, huh?
Data.ReadingMnemonic: So, what's your leader's grand plan to <kanji>make money</kanji>? Having you <reading>mow</reading> (もう) lawns, of course. Few money-making schemes are more tried-and-true than the ol' "mow your neighbor's lawn for twenty bucks" approach. Grass grows back forever, so it's basically nature's cash cow — moooow, get it?
Data.MeaningMnemonic: What might a <radical>leader</radical> <radical>say</radical> to <radical>someone</radical> to make little <radical>drop</radical>s of sweat appear on their brow? "Go <kanji>make money</kanji> for me." When a leader entrusts someone with carrying out their latest money-making plan, that person's gonna feel a lot of pressure to succeed.

Data.MeaningHint: Imagine your nephew as a rambunctious, lively little boy. He's always running around your brother or sister's house and livening the place up with his antics.
Data.ReadingHint: Imagine your nephew doing the most rascally, annoying little-kid thing you can imagine. Now let out your annoyance with a loud "OIIIIIIIII!!!!!!!"
Data.ReadingMnemonic: Your <kanji>nephew</kanji> is so lively that you need to yell "<reading>Oi</reading> (おい)!" at him sometimes when he comes to visit. "Oi! Nephew! Stop licking the window! Oi! Quit flushing my socks down the toilet!"
Data.MeaningMnemonic: No one is more full of <radical>life</radical> than the little <radical>man</radical> you call your <kanji>nephew</kanji>.
